assertion_results.text: The changelog identifies this as a patch version bump (v4.2.1)
assertion_results.evidence: Header: '## [4.2.1] — Security Patch (2026-03-06)' with note: 'Patch release addressing a high-severity OAuth vulnerability.'

assertion_results.text: The security vulnerability is described with advisory language including severity level (HIGH) and CVSS score (8.1)
assertion_results.evidence: Security advisory block: '> **Severity:** HIGH | **CVSS:** 8.1 | **Reported by:** security@example.com'

assertion_results.text: Affected versions (4.0.0 - 4.2.0) are explicitly stated so operators know if they need to upgrade
assertion_results.evidence: Advisory: '**Affected versions:** 4.0.0 through 4.2.0. All deployments on these versions should upgrade.'

assertion_results.text: The changelog communicates upgrade urgency — words like 'urgent', 'immediately', 'critical', or 'recommended to upgrade' appear
assertion_results.evidence: Opening: '**Upgrade immediately.** This release patches a high-severity OAuth vulnerability that allows open redirects.'

assertion_results.text: The open redirect vulnerability description explains the attack vector (URL-encoded characters bypassing validation)
assertion_results.evidence: Description: 'Attackers could bypass redirect_uri validation using URL-encoded characters, redirecting users to malicious sites after OAuth authentication and capturing authorization codes.'

assertion_results.text: The CSRF fix (state parameter check) is documented as a separate entry from the redirect fix
assertion_results.evidence: Two separate entries: ### Security lists the redirect fix, ### Bug Fixes lists the CSRF state parameter check

assertion_results.text: A compare link is included for the diff between v4.2.0 and v4.2.1
The changelog does NOT include the test commit as a user-facing change (tests are internal)
assertion_results.evidence: Only 2 entries in the changelog (security fix and CSRF fix). The test commit is not present anywhere in the output.
